tweets.text:
LinkedIn is not a professional network. It's a language.

I built a translator. Here's what it does. 🧵

veda.ng/lit

Nobody you know speaks like this in real life.
Yet somehow, on LinkedIn, it's the only language.

So I asked: what if we just... admitted that?

My personal favourite.

You took a nap. You are not optimising anything. You were tired.
LinkedIn disagrees.

The product launch one broke me.

"Had an intimate, high-signal gathering." Zero people showed up. The translator felt nothing.

I got arrested for fraud.
"Thrilled to announce I'm starting a new chapter."

At some point the platform became a parody of itself. I just made it official.

Try it: veda.ng/lit

Shopping used to mean: open browser, search, compare, click, form, checkout.

AI agents cut that to one step. You describe. They buy.

The agent is not a new channel. It is a new buyer. 🧵

During Cyber Week 2025, AI agents influenced 1 in 5 online orders globally.

Nearly half of US adults now use AI assistants regularly. But only 24% trust them to spend their money.

That gap is the entire business opportunity.

Every major company shipped a different answer to this in the last 12 months.

Google. OpenAI. Coinbase. Stripe. Mastercard. Visa.

Nobody agrees on how agents should pay. Six competing standards, all live, none dominant. The one that wins becomes invisible infrastructure.

When the buyer is an algorithm, your toothpaste ad does nothing.

Algorithms don't see display ads. They read structured data. Brand loyalty disappears when the agent picks the cheaper option with the same ingredients.

The infrastructure is built. The payment rails work.

What's still missing: only 24% of people trust an agent to spend their money. And legally, in most countries, an AI cannot sign a contract on your behalf.

Full essay: veda.ng/agentic-commerce

text:
Everyone argues about whether the datacenter buildout pays off.

Nobody is arguing about the third option.

Open source models match frontier models within 3-6 months of release. o1 came out in September 2024. DeepSeek-R1 matched it by January. GPT-4o in May. DeepSeek-V3 by December.

The gap has already closed.

Frontier model providers are bleeding money. OpenAI: $13B in revenue, $14B in projected losses for 2026. Prices will go up. When they do, running models locally stops being a preference and becomes the obvious call.

Apple is not building datacenters. Everyone says they're behind.

Their actual bet: let rivals burn cash training models, wait for open source to absorb those advances, build hardware that runs them. The M5 Max runs a 134B model locally today.

The cloud AI buildout may turn out to be R&D that open source inherits for free.

Frontier labs train the models. Open source copies them. Your laptop runs them.

No subscription. No data leaving your machine. No rate limits.

That future has no billionaires in it. That's why nobody talks about it.

Within a few years, most companies will have more AI agents running than human employees.

That is not a prediction. It is already starting.

When that happens, the old management model breaks.

Today you manage people. Check-ins, approvals, status updates. You stay on top because the number of decisions is manageable.

When thousands of agents are making thousands of decisions a day, you cannot manage that way. You have to manage by exception. Scan for what broke. Simulate what to do next.

The problem is there is nothing to simulate on.

Every CEO already runs what-ifs in their head. "If I cut prices here, what happens to margins there?" The thinking exists. It just lives in one person's head and walks out the door when they leave.

What needs to be built is that model in software. Autonomous cars had this problem first. Waymo does not train a car by telling it what to do. They build a world model. A simulation of the road where you run millions of what-ifs before the car touches real tarmac.

Business needs that. Something connected to every system in the company. Not a dashboard. Not an agent. Something that answers: if I do X, what happens next?

The tools exist for the left side of that image.

Nobody has built the right side yet.

Dario Amodei says AGI by 2026 or 2027. The co-founder of Epoch AI says 2045. Twenty years apart. Both have studied this longer than almost anyone.

This is not one of them being careless. The evidence does not pin it down.

The Metaculus community forecast shortened from 50 years to 5 years in just 5 years. Not because AI suddenly accelerated that much. Because people are updating fast on limited data.

The problem is the single number. When an expert says "AGI in 4 years," audiences hear "between 3 and 5." What the expert actually means: 10% chance next year, 25% chance by 2028, 50% chance by 2030, 10% chance not before 2050. Those are completely different situations to prepare for.

When 2027 comes and goes quietly, every outlet that ran "AI transforms everything by 2027" will say the whole field was wrong. The researchers never predicted 2027. They said it was one scenario in a distribution that runs from next year to next century.

Writing a book takes 5 years. There is roughly a 20% chance of transformative AI in the next 5 years. That means 80% of the expected value of that book survives.

The question is not whether timelines are short or long. It is how to act when both are live options.

When cars arrived, cities tried to fit them into streets built for horses. That did not work. They built new roads for cars. Those worked. But they destroyed neighborhoods in the process.

Terence Tao, the world's most celebrated living mathematician, thinks AI in mathematics is at the same point.

Math journals, papers, conferences, advisor-student relationships. All of it was built for humans working at human speed. AI gets from hypothesis to proof faster. But it skips everything that makes the proof valuable beyond the answer.

The author develops skills. The reader gets a map of the terrain. Dead ends get documented. Future researchers know where not to go.

An AI proof skips all of that. It arrives at the destination and has nothing to say about the road.

The mistake happening right now is trying to make AI write papers that look like human papers. That is fitting a car into a horse street. It looks right from a distance and does not work up close.

The better path: build new infrastructure for the new tool. Large-scale mathematical challenges verified by proof assistants. Libraries of rough machine-generated proofs that humans then refine into something worth reading.

The goal is not to replace the walkable city. It is to build the highway without demolishing the neighborhood.

We have not been good at that historically.
